comments.author: @WhereonEarth4K
comments.text:
👇 Everything we curated for this Madagascar episode. Books, wildlife guides, history, and Malagasy vanilla.

📚 BROWSE THE MADAGASCAR COLLECTION
Every book, field guide, and cultural item we'd recommend for this episode → https://amzn.to/3PUbdW5

🛍 SEE OUR FULL W.O.E. STOREFRONT
Every destination Collection in one place → https://www.amazon.com/shop/whereonearth

🎟 TOURS AND EXPERIENCES IN MADAGASCAR
Lemur and rainforest parks, the Avenue of the Baobabs, and the Tsingy stone forest → https://shorturl.at/qbmvY
Browse all Madagascar experiences → https://shorturl.at/ybPJ3

📖 PLAN YOUR MADAGASCAR TRIP
Bradt Madagascar, the most comprehensive guide to the island, 12th edition → https://amzn.to/3QAbwFA
Malagasy to English Dictionary and Phrasebook, a pocket language reference for travelers → https://amzn.to/4xkwtot

📚 GO DEEPER INTO MADAGASCAR
The Sloth Lemur's Song, an acclaimed natural history of the island from Gondwana to today by ecologist Alison Richard → https://amzn.to/49Pspmd
Beyond the Rice Fields by Naivo, the first novel from Madagascar ever translated into English → https://amzn.to/4ohz3HM

🌿 BRING MADAGASCAR HOME
Grade A Madagascar Bourbon vanilla beans, the island's signature export → https://amzn.to/49QZ36Y
Lemurs of Madagascar, the illustrated field guide to all one hundred plus species → https://amzn.to/4vAmdH1

comments.author: @TRUEGLOBAL-p7b
comments.text: The storytelling and visuals in this documentary are excellent.

comments.author: @beverlymendheim5691
comments.text: Aside from the mispronunciation of Malagasy words and its (hidden) connection to Africa, the documentary has some visual merit.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Appreciate the specific catch on pronunciation — Malagasy is genuinely tricky to get right, and it's worth us tightening. As for the African connection, that's real too — Madagascar sits at a real crossroads of Austronesian and African heritage.

comments.author: @PeterG-b5p
comments.text: To summarize, they're totally f***ed. And sure there are those to blame outside but they'll never take the blame of their mishandling.

comments.author: @PeterG-b5p
comments.text: The violence seen there means they're just part of humanity that likes to kill in the name of greed. Nothing changes.

comments.author: @PeterG-b5p
comments.text: Those ancient animals that were made extinct should Sue for reparations from humanity.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: If they could, they'd have a strong case. Thank you for watching.

comments.author: @DestinationDiscoveries-u4w
comments.text: Increíble documental. Madagascar es un lugar único, con una naturaleza y una biodiversidad impresionantes. 🦎🌴✨

comments.author: @Racr-j4o
comments.text: MADAGASCAR 🇲🇬 ISLANDS 🏝 WAS FOUND BY INDONESIA 🇮🇩 SEA EXPLORER

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Close — Madagascar was settled by Austronesian seafarers from the Indonesian archipelago roughly 1,500-2,000 years ago, one of the most remarkable ocean migrations in human history, though "found" undersells how long a journey it actually was.

comments.author: @Aventurex-686
comments.text: Madagascar feels like the continent that looked at the rest of the world and said, “I think I’ll do my own thing.” Millions of years later, it ended up with animals, plants, and landscapes that seem like they were designed by a creative team that ignored all the rules.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: "I think I'll do my own thing" — and then kept at it for longer than most continents have existed. That's exactly the tone. Thank you for watching. 🌿

comments.author: @ChanelExploreTravel4K
comments.text: Madagascar feels less like an island and more like a lost world. Millions of years of isolation created wildlife, landscapes, and ecosystems found nowhere else on Earth.

comments.author: @TruePlanetOffical
comments.text: Madagascar is literally its own continent — the lemurs, aye-aye, and baobabs are unreal. But losing half the forest in just 60 years? That’s heartbreaking.

comments.author: @EpicAdventureWorld
comments.text: If you could visit only one isolated island in the world, would you choose Madagascar? Why or why not?

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Hard to argue against it — nowhere else on Earth has that level of evolutionary isolation. What's pulling you toward it, or away from it?

replies.author: @beverlymendheim5691
replies.text: I've already visited this beautiful Island. I do some lectures about my visit.

comments.author: @UncutEarth4K
comments.text: This is an amazing documentary! I hope I can visit this country!

comments.author: @BestTravel2
comments.text: Madagascar is one of those places that seems completely unique. The wildlife, the landscapes, and the history of how it evolved in isolation make it feel like a world of its own.

comments.author: @wieb1000
comments.text: What's it to Us what they do to their Land? Surely not yours if you don't live there!

comments.author: @susannaemmerich1166
comments.text: And yet the population keep growing????????🙃🤔

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: It really is one of the paradoxes of the place — even with deforestation and resource strain, Madagascar's population growth rate is among the highest in the world, driven largely by high birth rates and a very young population.

comments.author: @thelanternmile
comments.text: The aye-aye gets killed because people think its finger is a death omen. That's wild. And the baobab avenue is basically a graveyard. This island is incredible and heartbreaking at the same time.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: "Incredible and heartbreaking at the same time" is exactly the frequency Madagascar operates on. The aye-aye chapter is one of the most quietly devastating in the whole film. Thank you for watching. 🌿
